worth_it_explanation: Curated deals decide how a publisher's first-party signals reach buyers and what price those signals command, so the packages built in Amazon Publisher Cloud sit between the inventory and the revenue it earns. A package mapped to the wrong inventory, or one left to run unchanged while the audience behind it ages, quietly underdelivers without anyone seeing a broken setting. The check is a review of packages a publisher already has in place, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and each finding points to a concrete package the publisher can remap or refresh.
intro: Amazon Publisher Cloud (APC) lets a publisher analyze its first-party signals alongside Amazon Ads insights to build curated deal packages that buyers activate in Amazon Demand-Side Platform (DSP). This subcategory is about the curated packages themselves, how they are scoped, which inventory they map to, and how current the signals behind them stay, rather than the open-auction yield mechanics covered elsewhere. These checks confirm that the packages are mapped to the inventory the publisher intends and that they are maintained on a schedule rather than left to drift.

audit_checks.title: Curated deal packages are not mapped to the intended inventory
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A curated package promises a buyer a defined audience on defined inventory, so a package that pulls the wrong ad units or the wrong signal segment delivers something other than what the buyer activated against. The mismatch shows up as weak delivery or buyer complaints rather than a visible error, and it can leave premium inventory packaged at a commodity rate or commodity inventory promised at a premium.

educate.base:
Amazon Publisher Cloud (APC) builds a curated deal by joining two things, the publisher's first-party signals and the inventory the deal is allowed to serve on. The publisher chooses the audience or outcome the package represents, APC packages the matching inventory, and the result is offered to buyers as a deal they activate in Amazon Demand-Side Platform (DSP). The value of the package rests on that mapping being correct since the deal sells a buyer a specific audience on a specific slice of inventory.

When the mapping drifts, the package stops describing what it delivers. A package meant to carry a high-intent audience on commercial pages may be scoped to ad units that also include thin editorial inventory, so the buyer pays for the audience but receives a blend. A package built around a first-party segment may point at a segment that was renamed or narrowed, so it serves a smaller or different audience than the deal name implies. In each case the buyer activated against one promise and the inventory delivers another, and the gap surfaces as underdelivery or a buyer raising a question rather than as a broken setting the publisher can see at a glance.

The mapping also carries a pricing consequence. A premium placement folded into a broad package can clear at the package rate rather than the rate the placement would command on its own, and commodity inventory promised inside a premium package can leave the buyer feeling the deal underdelivered. Either way the price stops matching the inventory.

A few signs point to packages that are not mapped to the intended inventory:
  • A package's ad unit or placement scope includes inventory the package was not meant to represent, e.g., editorial units inside a package sold as commercial
  • The first-party signal or audience behind a package no longer matches the segment named in the deal
  • Buyers report delivery that does not match what the package promised, or delivery falls short of the inventory the package should reach
You may want to confirm that each curated package's inventory scope and signal match the audience the deal name promises. One approach is to open each package, compare its mapped inventory and segment against the deal's stated intent, and correct any package where the two have diverged.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a curated package built around a section audience, e.g., a finance or travel vertical, should map to the ad units on those sections rather than a broad run-of-site scope. You may want to confirm that vertical packages are scoped to the sections they name, so a buyer activating a finance deal reaches finance inventory rather than a site-wide blend.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a curated package built around high-intent shoppers should map to product and category pages rather than editorial pages since the commercial inventory is what carries the intent the package sells. You may want to confirm that intent-based packages exclude the lower-intent editorial units, so the package delivers the audience the buyer paid for.

educate.learn_more_note: Amazon Publisher Services's detailed configuration documentation for Amazon Publisher Cloud packages and the Connections Marketplace is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each curated deal package in Amazon Publisher Cloud against its stated intent, checking which inventory and which first-party signal it maps to and whether those match the audience the deal name promises.

aps_console.label: Amazon Publisher Services console
steps: Open Amazon Publisher Cloud and list the active curated deal packages, recording each package name and the deal it backs.
For each package, open its configuration and note the inventory scope, e.g., the ad units or placements it serves on, and the first-party signal or audience segment it is built around.
Compare the mapped inventory and segment against the audience the package name and deal promise, and record any package where the scope or signal has diverged from the stated intent.
Ask the publisher whether any buyer has reported delivery that did not match a package, and record which package and what the buyer described.

amazon_dsp_reporting.label: Amazon DSP deal reporting
steps: Pull delivery for each curated deal by deal identifier, with impressions, spend, and any available reach or audience match metric.
Compare the inventory and audience a deal actually reached against the package's stated scope to see whether delivery matches the promise.
Note any deal that underdelivers or reaches inventory outside its intended scope, which points back to a package that needs remapping.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per curated package, the inventory scope and first-party signal it maps to alongside the audience the deal name promises. A package whose mapped inventory and signal match its stated intent passes. A package whose scope includes unintended inventory, or whose signal no longer matches the named segment, is a fail recorded with the package name and the way the mapping has diverged.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each package's mapped inventory and signal against its stated intent above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more curated deal packages on {{site_name}} are not mapped to the inventory the deal promises, so a buyer activating the package in Amazon Demand-Side Platform (DSP) receives something other than what the package name describes. A package whose scope includes unintended inventory, or whose first-party signal no longer matches the named segment, delivers a different audience or price than the buyer expected, and the gap surfaces as underdelivery rather than a visible error. {{voice_recommend}} opening each package in Amazon Publisher Cloud (APC), comparing its mapped inventory and signal against the deal's stated intent, and correcting any package where the two have diverged. After the remap, recheck delivery by deal to confirm each package reaches the inventory it promises.

audit_checks.title: Curated deal packages are left to run without maintenance
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A curated package built on first-party signals reflects the audience that existed when it was created, and that audience shifts as visitors and content change. A package left unchanged for many months keeps serving against an aging definition, so it can shrink, drift off the audience it names, or sit idle behind a deal no buyer activates, each of which lowers the revenue the package returns without any setting appearing broken.

educate.base:
A curated deal package is built at a point in time. Amazon Publisher Cloud (APC) joins the publisher's first-party signals with Amazon Ads insights to define the audience, and that definition reflects the visitors, the content, and the buyer demand that existed when the package was created. None of those hold still. Visitors turn over, content shifts, and the buyers interested in a package change as their own campaigns change, so a package is accurate when built and drifts from there.

Left to run without maintenance, a package degrades in more than one way. The first-party segment behind it can shrink as the audience that defined it moves on, so the package reaches fewer impressions than the deal implies. The audience can drift off the theme the package names as new content pulls in different visitors. And a package can simply go idle, sitting behind a deal that no buyer has activated in months while newer packages carry the demand. In each case the package keeps existing, which is why the decay is easy to miss, but it stops earning what it could.

Maintenance is a review on a schedule rather than a rebuild. A publisher can read each package's recent delivery and the size of the signal behind it, refresh or rescope the packages whose audiences have shifted, and retire the packages that no buyer activates so the active set reflects current demand. Treating the package set as something reviewed on a cadence keeps it matched to the audience and the buyers that exist now.

A few signs point to packages left without maintenance:
  • Packages exist that have not been reviewed or updated in many months, with no record of when they were last checked
  • The first-party signal behind a package has shrunk well below its original size, so the package reaches far fewer impressions than the deal implies
  • Packages sit behind deals that no buyer has activated recently, taking up the active set without earning revenue
You may want to confirm that curated packages are reviewed on a regular cadence rather than left to drift. One approach is to set a recurring review where each package's recent delivery and signal size are read, the drifted packages are refreshed, and the idle packages are retired.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a package built around a seasonal or event audience, e.g., an election or a sports season, can outlive the moment that defined it and keep running against an audience that has moved on. You may want to review time-bound packages on a cadence so they are refreshed or retired once the audience behind them has shifted.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a package built around a product interest can drift as the catalog and the shopping audience change across a season. You may want to review intent-based packages regularly so the signal behind each still reflects the products and shoppers it names rather than an audience from an earlier season.

learn_more.source_title: Prebid.js First Party Data for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: Amazon Publisher Services's detailed configuration documentation for maintaining Amazon Publisher Cloud packages is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each curated deal package for when it was last updated, the current size of the first-party signal behind it, and whether buyers are still activating it, to find packages that have drifted or gone idle.

aps_console.label: Amazon Publisher Services console
steps: Open Amazon Publisher Cloud and list the active curated deal packages with the date each was created or last updated.
For each package, note the current size of the first-party signal or audience behind it and compare it against the size when the package was built, where that is available.
Record any package that has not been reviewed in many months, any whose signal has shrunk well below its original size, and any that appears idle.
Ask the publisher whether a review cadence exists for the package set, and record how often packages are checked and when the last review happened.

amazon_dsp_reporting.label: Amazon DSP deal reporting
steps: Pull delivery by deal identifier across a recent window, with impressions and spend per deal.
Identify deals with little or no recent activation, which point to packages that buyers are no longer using.
Note packages whose delivery has fallen steadily over time, which points to a signal or audience that has shrunk or drifted.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per curated package, when it was last reviewed, the current size of the signal behind it, and its recent activation. A package set reviewed on a regular cadence, with drifted packages refreshed and idle packages retired, passes. A set where packages run for many months without review, where signals have shrunk well below their original size, or where idle packages remain in the active set is a fail recorded with the packages affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each package's last review date, signal size, and recent activation above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that curated deal packages on {{site_name}} are running without a maintenance cadence, so the audiences behind them have been left to drift. A package built in Amazon Publisher Cloud (APC) reflects the audience that existed when it was created, and the first-party signal behind it can shrink, drift off the theme it names, or go idle behind a deal no buyer activates, each of which lowers the revenue the package returns without any setting appearing broken. {{voice_recommend}} setting a recurring review where each package's recent delivery and signal size are read, refreshing the packages whose audiences have shifted, and retiring the packages that buyers no longer activate. After the first review, hold the cadence so the active set keeps matching the audience and the buyers that exist now.
